stories.title: CJ Cup Byron Nelson Opens — Scheffler Seeks Response at TPC Craig Ranch
stories.angle: Scheffler returns to his home turf four days after a quiet T-14 finish at the PGA Championship. This is his bounce-back opportunity on a course he knows well.

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
The CJ Cup Byron Nelson is underway. $10.3M on the line at TPC Craig Ranch.

Scheffler defends after a quiet T-14 at Aronimink. He's grouped with Koepka and Si Woo Kim this morning.

World No. 1 back on familiar turf. 🏌️

#GolfFanrecap #ByronNelson #PGATour

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
5 players withdrew from the Byron Nelson field following Aaron Rai's PGA Championship win.

A lot has changed in a week. A new major champion. A reshuffled field. Golf moves fast.

Round 1 underway in McKinney, Texas. #GolfFanrecap #ByronNelson

facebook.long_form:
Professional golf moved to Texas today, and the backdrop couldn't be more compelling.

The CJ Cup Byron Nelson opened Round 1 at TPC Craig Ranch in McKinney with $10.3 million in prize money on the line and Scottie Scheffler, the world No. 1, returning to defend the title he won here last year. Scheffler — grouped with Brooks Koepka and Si Woo Kim for the opening round — arrives just four days after a quiet T-14 at Aronimink, where his attempt to repeat as PGA champion fell short in the final round.

Five players withdrew from the Byron Nelson field in the days following Aaron Rai's PGA Championship victory last Sunday. Scheduling adjustments, exemption changes, and the general wave of post-major reshuffling that follows a surprise major winner have altered what was already a strong field.

Scheffler is the man to beat. He won here in 2025, knows TPC Craig Ranch as well as any player in the world, and has the kind of bounce-back capacity that separates world-class players from everyone else. After Aronimink, he needs rounds, rhythm, and results. Craig Ranch is the place to find all three.

Round 2 tomorrow. Leaderboard takes shape.
facebook.image_caption:
The CJ Cup Byron Nelson is LIVE at TPC Craig Ranch. $10.3M on the table.

Scheffler defends. The field is stacked. Round 1 underway in McKinney, Texas. 🏌️

#GolfFanrecap #ByronNelson #PGATour

article.headline: CJ Cup Byron Nelson Opens: Scheffler Seeks Bounce-Back Win at TPC Craig Ranch

stories.title: Aaron Rai's New World — The Perks of Winning a Major
stories.angle: Historic context: first Englishman to win the PGA Championship since 1919; first player of Indian heritage to win any men's major. The legacy of what Rai achieved is still being understood.

x_posts.label: Post 2
x_posts.text:
Aaron Rai won $3.69 million at Aronimink.

But the exemptions are where it gets life-changing:
• Lifetime PGA Championship entry
• 5 years: Masters, US Open, Open Championship, The Players

One tournament. An entirely different career. #GolfFanrecap #AaronRai

facebook.long_form:
When Aaron Rai holed that nearly 70-foot putt on the 17th green at Aronimink last Sunday, he wasn't just winning a golf tournament. He was rewriting his professional future.

The $3.69 million prize money is the most visible number — and the one that, according to reports, completely transformed his 2026 earnings picture on its own. But the exemptions that accompany a PGA Championship title are where the lasting value lives.

Rai earned a lifetime exemption into the PGA Championship. He also received five-year invitations to the Masters, the US Open, the Open Championship, and The Players Championship — the four most prestigious events in the sport, now guaranteed to him through at least 2031. For a player who had not previously earned full-time invitation status at all of these events, it is a structural change in his career, not just a statistical one.

The historical context adds meaning to every start he makes from here. He is the first Englishman to win the PGA Championship since 1919. He is the first player of Indian heritage to win any men's major championship. Born in Wolverhampton to a family with roots in India and Kenya, Rai described his identity, his upbringing, and the people who shaped him as central to what the victory meant.

Those things don't fade when the trophy is put away. They travel with him.
facebook.image_caption:
$3.69 million. A lifetime PGA exemption. Five years at the Masters, US Open, Open, and The Players.

Aaron Rai won more than a tournament at Aronimink. He changed his entire career.

#GolfFanrecap #AaronRai #PGAChampionship

article.headline: What Aaron Rai Won Beyond the Wanamaker Trophy: The Career-Changing Perks of a PGA Championship

stories.title: McIlroy's Grand Slam Wait — What Comes Next After Aronimink
stories.angle: McIlroy carries three legs of the Grand Slam going into the US Open. The PGA miss hurts but the US Open at a course like Oakmont or Pinehurst could set the stage for another attempt.

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text:
Masters. US Open. Open Championship. ✓✓✓

PGA Championship: T-7 at Aronimink. Five strokes back.

McIlroy's Grand Slam bid lives on. Next stop: Memorial Tournament, June 4-7. Then the US Open.

#GolfFanrecap #McIlroy #GrandSlam

facebook.long_form:
Rory McIlroy walked away from Aronimink Golf Club on Sunday with three legs of the Grand Slam — and without the fourth.

McIlroy won the 2026 Masters at Augusta National earlier this season, bringing his major collection to three of the four required for the Grand Slam: Masters, US Open, and The Open Championship. He came to the 108th PGA Championship with a chance to complete it. He finished T-7, five strokes behind Aaron Rai, in a final round that never gave him the openings he needed.

He will not attempt to recover quickly at the Byron Nelson or the Charles Schwab Challenge. His schedule puts him next on the tee at the Memorial Tournament hosted by Jack Nicklaus at Muirfield Village in Ohio, June 4-7. That becomes the bridge to the US Open, which is now the event where McIlroy's Grand Slam pursuit resumes.

The wait is difficult, but the opportunity remains. Three legs of the Grand Slam is exactly where McIlroy stood when he reached Augusta earlier this year. He converted that one. The US Open is a championship he has won before. The history is not closed.
facebook.image_caption:
Masters ✓. US Open ✓. Open Championship ✓.

PGA Championship: T-7. Five back.

McIlroy's Grand Slam wait goes on. Next big chance: the US Open. #GolfFanrecap #McIlroy

article.headline: McIlroy's Grand Slam Wait Continues: Three Legs, One Miss, and the Road to the US Open

stories.title: Scheffler's Failed Defense and What It Means at Byron Nelson
stories.angle: Does a quiet major performance change momentum? Scheffler has bounced back from disappointing majors before. His familiarity with Craig Ranch makes this a logical spot for a reset.

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
Scheffler led the PGA Championship after Round 1 at -3.

He finished T-14 at -2. Couldn't find the closing magic.

Now he's back at TPC Craig Ranch defending the Byron Nelson. This is his course, his tournament, his reset.

#GolfFanrecap #Scheffler #ByronNelson

facebook.long_form:
Scottie Scheffler led the 2026 PGA Championship after Round 1. He did not win it.

The world No. 1 shot 3-under in the opening round at Aronimink, finding himself at the top of a loaded leaderboard. Over the following three days, the course tightened, the competition intensified, and Scheffler — who has made a habit of producing closing magic in major situations — couldn't find his best when it mattered most. He finished tied for 14th at 2-under, five strokes behind Aaron Rai.

That is not who Scheffler is. He has won here at TPC Craig Ranch. He knows this course. And golf history is full of examples of great players who absorb a quiet major finish and then produce something commanding in the weeks that follow.

Today was the first day of that process. Scheffler opened Round 1 of the CJ Cup Byron Nelson alongside Brooks Koepka and Si Woo Kim at TPC Craig Ranch — the same course where he lifted the Byron Nelson trophy last year. If the reset takes hold, Craig Ranch will show it quickly.
facebook.image_caption:
He led the PGA Championship after R1. Finished T-14.

Now Scheffler is back at TPC Craig Ranch, where he won the Byron Nelson in 2025.

World No. 1 finds his reset. 🏌️ #GolfFanrecap #Scheffler #ByronNelson

article.headline: Scheffler's Quiet Major Defense and the Byron Nelson Reset: Back Where It Started

stories.title: LIV Golf Virginia — Lucas Herbert's Breakthrough
stories.angle: Herbert is relatively under the radar even within LIV. A four-shot win at a 72-hole event is a convincing statement of form. DeChambeau's T3 keeps him near the top of the LIV standings.

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
Lucas Herbert just won his first LIV Golf title.

24-under 264 at Trump National DC. Four shots over Sergio Garcia. Bryson DeChambeau third.

LIV Golf Korea is next: May 28-31, Asiad Country Club in Busan.

#GolfFanrecap #LIVGolf

facebook.long_form:
Lucas Herbert is a LIV Golf winner.

The 29-year-old Australian claimed his first LIV title at Trump National Golf Club in Sterling, Virginia, finishing at 24-under par 264 — four shots clear of Sergio Garcia, who shot 3-under in the final round to finish second. Bryson DeChambeau came in third.

Herbert's win is significant not just for the player, but for what it says about the depth developing in LIV Golf. He is not one of the circuit's marquee names — Garcia and DeChambeau carry more recognition, and Dustin Johnson and Brooks Koepka dominate the discussion around LIV's legacy players. Herbert has operated quietly in the background while improving. Virginia showed that quiet improvement had become genuine elite-level performance.

The LIV circuit now heads to South Korea for the next event: LIV Golf Korea at Asiad Country Club in Busan, May 28-31. DeChambeau, Garcia, and Herbert will all be in the field, carrying different forms of motivation into a venue that none of them have played before.
facebook.image_caption:
Lucas Herbert — 24-under. Four shots over Garcia. First LIV Golf win.

The tour heads to Korea next: May 28-31 in Busan. #GolfFanrecap #LIVGolf

article.headline: Herbert's LIV Golf Breakthrough: Virginia Win Opens New Chapter as Tour Heads to Korea
